title: Budget war on Boomers won’t make young better off
full_text: Other Editions 07 May 2026 Economy Budget war on Boomers won’t make young better off afr.com The AFR View 1 May 2026 07:33 GMT 939 words English AFNROL Copyright 2026. Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited. Let’s consider Anthony Albanese’s spin on the likely paring back of the capital gains tax discount and limiting the negative gearing tax break for property investors in the May 12 federal budget. The prime minister tells AFR Weekend that winding back these tax concessions would be about “aspiration and opportunity going forward for all”, not an anti-Baby Boomer budget waging war on wealthier older Australians. Prime Minister Anthony Albanese is right to suggest that younger generations’ sense of aspiration and opportunity continues to centre on the dream of owning their own home. Oscar Colman The proposed changes to CGT and negative gearing were both part of Labor’s bigger taxing policies that contributed to Bill Shorten’s defeat at the 2019 election. Is old wine – the politics of envy – now being presented in new bottles labelled intergenerational inequality? Last August’s economic reform summit in Canberra highlighted the long-term unsustainability of the nation’s tax policy settings. Younger workers face paying higher tax rates in the coming decades to fund rising government spending, especially in areas such as health and aged care for older generations. The correct policy response to this generational dilemma should be serious structural tax reform. Shifting more of the tax burden onto consumption – such as by raising the GST rate or broadening its base – would make lower taxes on personal and business income possible. The work- and investment-incentive-sharpening effects would also help grow a bigger pie and make the costs of an ageing society more affordable. Genuine reform also needs to start on the spending rather than the revenue side of the budget, as fiscal restraint creates room to deliver lower, simpler, and fairer taxes for all. “Removing tax concessions that disproportionately benefit wealthy Boomers, who have substantial housing, superannuation and other assets, won’t automatically make younger ones better off.” But instead of embracing an ambitious fiscal repair and taxation agenda in this budget, Labor is presenting its long-desired changes to investment taxes as a means of levelling up between generations. That appears to have already begun with the government’s abolition of the seniors’ rebate on private health insurance premiums. The political context is a changing electoral landscape. Millennials and Gen Z are now the fastest-growing and most influential voting blocs. Rapid rises in house prices and younger people struggling to get on to the property ladder have sharpened the sense of a conflict between the generations. Globally, the inability to achieve home ownership is driving younger voters to disproportionately support left-wing, populist parties, such as the Greens in Australia. The prime minister is right to suggest that younger generations’ sense of aspiration and opportunity continues to centre on the dream of owning their own home. However, solutions to home affordability lie mostly on the supply side, such as removing planning and approval policies for new builds and medium-density housing. The claims made about intergenerational housing and wealth divides are not clear-cut. An Actuaries Institute report released on Thursday showed that the decline in home ownership among 25-34-year-olds has partially reversed over the last five years, bucking a 40-year downward trend. That may be partly due to some younger buyers receiving help from the “bank of mum and dad” to buy a home. And according to the E61 Institute, the average wealth of a 35-year-old today is broadly in line with that of earlier generations at the same age. Younger people may be buying houses later, but they will also build up significant super balances over their careers, and many stand to benefit from inheriting significant sums from their parents. The idea that the once-politically dominant Baby Boomers had it good for a long time, and now it’s their turn to cough up some cash, may be seductive. Contrast the Boomer generation, which received “free” university education, with those who today have HECS debts for degrees whose value is now threatened by the AI revolution’s elimination of white-collar jobs. Common sense says that, of course, there are disparities in wealth between older and younger age groups that reflect their different life stages. Zero-sum thinking about a generational war is simplistic. Removing tax concessions that disproportionately benefit wealthy Boomers, who have substantial housing, superannuation and other assets, won’t automatically make younger ones better off. Winding back the CGT discount and negative gearing would send a symbolically important signal that the political system is willing to respond to younger voters’ concerns. But modelling by the Grattan Institute suggests house prices would only end up 1 per cent to 2 per cent lower than they otherwise would be. There is also the risk that higher taxes on housing investment could reduce new supply and put upward pressure on rents and property prices. Moreover, if the CGT and negative gearing changes only apply to new investments, the relatively small amount of revenue raised will not create significant fiscal headroom for more meaningful reforms that deliver bigger benefits to younger workers. It is imperative that the government addresses bracket creep that confiscates more of workers’ income due to inflation and wage rises, forcing them to pay higher marginal tax rates. Treasurer Jim Chalmers continues to promise he will deliver a reform budget on May 12 that will improve intergenerational equity. But cherry-picked changes to investment taxes, which may be worthwhile, will in the absence of broader tax reform, do little to substantially improve the financial circumstances of the young. summary: The upcoming federal budget is expected to propose cuts to capital gains tax discounts and negative gearing, aimed at addressing intergenerational inequality. However, experts argue that these changes may not significantly benefit younger Australians and could lead to unintended consequences in the housing market. A broader structural tax reform is suggested as a more effective solution to improve financial circumstances for younger generations.
publication:
Budget war on Boomers won’t make young better off
afr.com01 May 2026 07:33 GMT939 wordsThe AFR View

Let’s consider Anthony Albanese’s spin on the likely paring back of the capital gains tax discount and limiting the negative gearing tax break for property investors in the May 12 federal budget.
heading: Actuaries
key_points: Proposed tax changes may not improve financial outcomes for younger Australians.
The government is under pressure to address intergenerational wealth disparities.
Broader tax reform is needed to effectively tackle the challenges faced by younger workers.
relevance: Understanding the implications of tax policy changes is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as they navigate the evolving economic landscape.
